title: Is My Kundalini Awakening Real or Am I Making It Up?

body:
Is My Kundalini Awakening Real or Am I Making It Up?

You're here, aren't you? Probably staring at your screen at some ungodly hour, heart pounding, mind racing. You've been through it, or you're in the thick of it right now. That feeling like your body's betraying you, your mind's playing tricks, and the very fabric of reality is unraveling. You're wondering if you're going crazy, if you've dreamt it all up, or if maybe, just maybe, there's something to this "kundalini" thing everyone talks about. I get it. I've been there. That gnawing doubt, the terror that you've somehow broken your brain beyond repair. It’s a lonely place, isn't it? You feel like you can't tell anyone, because who'd believe you? Who wouldn't just tell you to see a shrink and get on some meds?

Here's the thing: you're not making it up. You're not crazy. What you're experiencing is real. The sensations, the visions, the emotional tidal waves - they're all happening. Your body and your consciousness are undergoing a massive, violent, and often terrifying overhaul. It's not a gentle breeze; it's a hurricane tearing through your entire being. And yeah, it's messy. It's confusing. It makes you question everything you thought you knew about yourself and the world. But that doesn't make it any less real. In fact, that's exactly how it feels when your deepest energy starts to move in ways it wasn't designed to in our modern, disconnected lives. You're not alone in this, even if it feels like you're the only one on the planet going through it. I promise you that.

The Unmistakable Signs You're Not Imagining It

Let's talk about what's actually happening. You're probably experiencing a whole host of bizarre symptoms that don't fit neatly into any medical diagnosis you've tried to Google. Doctors often don't get it; they're looking for something that fits their charts, and this doesn't. You're not going to find a blood test for kundalini. So, you're left feeling even more isolated, right? Like you're a medical mystery, or worse, just a hypochondriac with an overactive imagination. But these symptoms, they're the language your body is using to tell you something monumental is happening. It's not a whisper; it's a scream. And you're hearing it.

I'll tell you what I went through, and what countless others have described. You'll probably recognize some of this. You might have felt intense heat, like fire burning up your spine, or icy cold rushes that make you shiver even when it's warm. Maybe you've had involuntary movements, tremors, jerks, or even felt like you were being stretched or contorted. They call these kriyas. They can be subtle, like a twitch, or violent enough to throw you across a room. They're not voluntary; you can't stop them, can you? That's a big clue right there. Your body is doing things outside your conscious control. That's not "making it up." That's your nervous system doing some serious rewiring.

Then there's the sensory overload. Sounds becoming too loud, colors too bright, smells too intense. You might feel like your skin is crawling, or that electricity is running through your veins. You might hear internal sounds - buzzing, humming, clicks, even music or voices that aren't there externally. Your sleep patterns are probably a mess, too. Insomnia, vivid dreams, waking up feeling more exhausted than when you went to bed. You might even experience out-of-body sensations, feeling disconnected from your physical form, or like you're floating. These aren't hallucinations in the typical sense; they're alterations in perception caused by heightened energetic activity.

The Emotional Rollercoaster and Psychic Shifts

Oh, the emotions. You're probably riding a wild, unpredictable rollercoaster. One minute you're euphoric, feeling boundless love and connection, the next you're plunged into the deepest despair, fear, or rage you've ever known. Old traumas, forgotten memories - they all come bubbling up to the surface. It's like your subconscious is being emptied out, whether you want it to be or not. You might find yourself crying uncontrollably, or laughing hysterically for no apparent reason. It feels like you're losing your grip on your own emotional stability, doesn't it? That's because you are, in a way. Your emotional body is being cleansed, and it's not a gentle process. It's like a pressure cooker releasing decades of stored pain and unresolved feelings.

And then there are the more subtle, but equally jarring, changes. You might find your intuition suddenly supercharged, or you start having prophetic dreams. You might become sensitive to other people's energies, picking up on their emotions or even their thoughts. You might see auras, or flashes of light, or develop a sudden interest in spirituality that feels like it came out of nowhere. These aren't things you're imagining; they're genuine shifts in your perception and your energetic field. It's like your psychic antennae are suddenly turned up to eleven, and you're picking up signals you never could before. It can be overwhelming, I know. It's like suddenly being able to hear every conversation in a crowded room when you used to only hear your own thoughts. It's a lot to process.

Look, if you're nodding along to any of this, if you're feeling that jolt of recognition, then you're not making it up. These experiences are too specific, too intense, and too consistent across millions of accounts throughout history for them to be mere imagination. They're real, and they're happening to you. You're not alone. There are resources out there, people who understand. I found a lot of comfort and validation reading books like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link). It helped me realize I wasn't just losing my mind. It gave me language for what I was feeling.

Why the Doubt? The Mind's Resistance

So, if it's so real, why does your mind keep telling you you're faking it, or making it up? Why that persistent, nagging doubt? Well, your mind, your ego, it's built on a foundation of what it knows, what it can categorize, what it can control. This whole kundalini thing? It defies all of that. It's chaotic, unpredictable, and completely outside the realm of your usual conscious control. Your ego feels threatened because it's losing its grip. It wants to rationalize, to explain, to fit it into a neat little box. When it can't, it defaults to denial, to telling you you're crazy or imagining things. It's a defense mechanism, a way for your mind to try and maintain some semblance of order in a world that suddenly feels like it's tilting on its axis.

Also, let's be honest, our society isn't exactly set up to understand or support this kind of experience. We're taught to be rational, logical, to trust what we can see and touch. Anything outside of that is labeled "woo-woo" or "mental illness." So, you're constantly bombarded with messages that invalidate what you're going through. You internalize that, right? You start to doubt yourself because everyone around you (or at least, the mainstream narrative) is telling you it's impossible. It's hard to hold onto your truth when the world seems to be screaming otherwise. But here's the thing: your inner experience is valid, regardless of what anyone else thinks or says. Your body doesn't lie. Your nervous system doesn't make things up for kicks. These are genuine physiological and energetic shifts.

Another reason for doubt is the sheer intensity of it. It's so far beyond normal experience that it feels unreal. How can something so powerful, so life-changing, be happening without you actively doing anything to cause it? It feels like you've stumbled into a secret world, and your mind is struggling to catch up. It's like trying to describe a new color to someone who's only ever seen black and white. It's just outside their frame of reference. And sometimes, you're your own worst enemy, constantly questioning, constantly analyzing. But what if you just accepted it? What if you allowed yourself to believe that this incredible, terrifying, beautiful thing is actually happening?

The Fear of the Unknown

The fear of the unknown is a huge driver of doubt. You don't know what's happening, you don't know why, and you certainly don't know when it's going to stop or what the end result will be. That uncertainty is terrifying. Your mind craves certainty, structure, predictability. Kundalini is none of those things. It's wild, untamed energy, and it's doing what it needs to do, regardless of your plans or preferences. This fear can manifest as intense anxiety, panic attacks, or even a feeling of impending doom. It makes you want to retreat, to shut it all down, to pretend it's not happening. But you can't, can you? It's like trying to stop a tidal wave with your bare hands. The energy is moving, and your best bet isn't to fight it, but to learn how to ride the wave.

I know it feels like you're losing control. Because you are. And that's okay. Sometimes, losing control is the only way to gain a deeper, more authentic control over your life. It's counterintuitive, but trust me on this. This process is stripping away everything that isn't truly you, all the masks, all the conditioning, all the false beliefs. It's painful, yes. It's disorienting. But it's also incredibly liberating. It's preparing you for a deeper connection to your true self, to something bigger than you can even imagine right now. You're not making it up. You're just experiencing a reality that most people aren't aware of, and your mind is struggling to catch up. Reading resources like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) can really help you get a handle on what's going on, and realize you're not an anomaly.

Finding Your Ground: What to Do When Doubt Creeps In

So, you're convinced it's real - at least for a moment - and then the doubt creeps back in, whispering its insidious lies. How do you deal with it? How do you stay grounded when your world feels like it's spinning? It's not easy, but it's possible. It takes conscious effort, patience, and a willingness to be kind to yourself. You're in a marathon, not a sprint, and there will be good days and bad days. The goal isn't to eliminate doubt entirely - that's probably impossible - but to learn to acknowledge it, and then gently redirect your focus back to your experience and your truth.

  • Acknowledge the doubt, don't fight it: When that voice says, "You're making this up," don't argue with it. Just say, "Okay, mind, I hear you. You're scared, and you want to make sense of this." Then, gently bring your attention back to your body, to the sensations you're feeling. The physical reality of your experience is undeniable, even if your mind tries to deny it.
  • Connect with your body: This is crucial. Your body is the anchor. When your mind is spiraling, bring your awareness to your breath, to the feeling of your feet on the ground, to the warmth of your hands. Simple grounding exercises can make a huge difference. Go for a walk in nature, feel the sun on your skin, take a warm bath. Anything that brings you back into your physical presence.
  • Journal your experiences: Write it all down. Every bizarre sensation, every intense emotion, every strange dream. Not only does this help you process what's happening, but it also creates a tangible record. When doubt creeps in, you can look back at your journal and see the consistent patterns, the progression, the undeniable reality of your journey. It's hard to deny something you've meticulously documented for weeks or months.
  • Seek out others who understand: This is a big one. You need to know you're not alone. Find online communities, support groups, or even just one person who has been through something similar. Sharing your experiences and hearing others validate them is incredibly powerful. It helps dismantle that feeling of isolation and abnormality.
  • Educate yourself: Read books about kundalini and spiritual awakenings. The more you understand the process, the less scary and mysterious it becomes. Knowledge is power, especially when you're dealing with something so intense. The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is another excellent resource, offering practical guidance and understanding.
  • Practice self-compassion: You're going through something incredibly difficult. Be kind to yourself. You wouldn't judge a friend for struggling with a serious illness, so don't judge yourself for struggling with this. Rest when you need to, ask for help, and don't push yourself too hard. This isn't a race; it's a process of deep transformation.

Embracing the Reality of Your Awakening

The truth is, you're not making it up. You're undergoing an intense, involuntary spiritual awakening. It's a real phenomenon, documented across cultures and throughout history. It's challenging, yes, and it can feel like you're losing your mind, but you're not. You're actually finding a deeper, more authentic version of yourself. This isn't a delusion; it's a deep shift in consciousness. You're experiencing a recalibration of your entire being, from your physical body to your deepest spiritual core.

I know it's scary. I know you're tired. I know you just want it to stop sometimes, or at least to make sense. And it will, in time. The intensity will ebb and flow. You'll learn to integrate these experiences. You'll develop new ways of understanding yourself and the world. This isn't a punishment; it's an initiation. It's a call to wake up, to shed the old, and to step into something new and expansive. Your doubt is just your mind's way of trying to protect you from something it doesn't understand. But you, your deeper self, you know this is real. You know it in your bones, in the very core of your being.

Trust that knowing. Trust your experience. It's okay to be scared, it's okay to feel overwhelmed, but don't let the doubt convince you that what you're going through isn't real. It is. It's intensely, unequivocally real. And you're strong enough to get through it. You wouldn't be experiencing this if you weren't. You've got this, even when it feels like you don't. Keep reading, keep connecting, keep breathing. You're on a journey that will change everything, and that's not something you could ever make up. For further reading, and to understand the broader context of these experiences, I highly recommend The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link). It puts these intense states into a much larger, more understandable picture. It helps you see that you're not alone in these wild, inner landscapes. Does that land?

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
